title: Pine spice rack
subtitle: Wall-mounted seasoned-pine rack for small jars
excerpt: A simple wall-mounted spice rack in seasoned pine. Two shelves, a back board, two side boards joined with rebates and panel nails, sized for the standard small spice jar. A first joinery project that uses a hand-cut rebate and finishes with shellac.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for the hand-cut rebate sequence. Carpentry for Boys (1914) on small wall-shelf joinery.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried (or kiln-dried) timber stable enough for indoor use, with moisture content in the 8 to 12 percent range.
A step-shaped notch cut along the edge of a board into which another board sits. The simplest joint for shelf-and-side construction.
A small wooden or metal tool with a sharp pin set at an adjustable distance from a fence; scores a line parallel to an edge.

content.text: A wall-mounted spice rack is one of the most useful small joinery projects: two shelves on a back board, sized to take the standard 60 mm diameter spice jar. The construction is rebates and nails, the wood is
seasoned
pine boards from any timber yard. This project introduces the hand-cut rebate and the try-square sequence that underlies all small box and shelf work.
The wood

PAR (planed all round) pine, 18 mm thick. Buy three lengths: one back board 300 mm by 220 mm, two side boards 220 mm by 75 mm, two shelves 280 mm by 75 mm. PAR pine is sold from any timber yard or DIY shop and arrives ready to use. Moisture content is 8 to 12 percent if stored indoors. Avoid boards with shake (long-grain splits visible on the face) or large knots in the rebate lines.
Tools
A hand saw for crosscuts, a 12 mm bench chisel for the rebates, a
marking gauge

and marking knife for scribing the rebate shoulders, a try-square for marking the cross cuts, a carpenter's pencil, and a cordless drill for the wall-fixing holes. Shellac for the finish. No power tools beyond the drill.
Workspace

A solid bench or kitchen table; the chisel work needs the board to sit firm. A workbench vice is helpful but not essential; a G-clamp securing the board to the table works for a piece this size. Good overhead light.
Method

Cut all five boards to length with the hand saw, checking each cut with the try-square. Back board: 300 mm by 220 mm. Sides: 220 mm by 75 mm. Shelves: 280 mm by 75 mm. Light sand all edges with 120 grit.

Mark the shelf positions on the side boards. From the top end of each side, measure down 30 mm to the top edge of the upper shelf, then 90 mm to the top edge of the lower shelf. With the try-square, draw a line across each side board at each shelf position. Repeat on both side boards.
Mark a
rebate

on each shelf line. Set the marking gauge to 18 mm (the thickness of the shelf board). With the gauge fence held against the back edge of the side board, score a line parallel to the back along each shelf position; the rebate sits between the cross-line and this gauge line. Set the gauge to 9 mm (half the side board thickness) and score the rebate depth from the inside face.

Cut the rebate. With the side board flat on the bench, saw down the cross-line shoulder to the depth line. Then chisel the waste out from the back edge to the saw cut, working in small bites with the bench chisel held bevel-up. Pare the rebate floor smooth and check it is flat with the back of the chisel against it.
Repeat the rebate cuts for the second shelf position on the same side board, then both rebates on the second side board. Four rebates in total; each takes 15 minutes once you have the rhythm.

Test-fit the shelves into the rebates. The shelf should slide in with light hand pressure and sit flush with the back of the side. If it is tight, pare the rebate floor with light chisel cuts; if it is loose, the rebate is too deep and the next project will be tighter.

Glue and nail the assembly. Spread a thin film of PVA wood glue on the rebate floors, slot the shelves into the side boards, then sandwich the back board against the back edges of the sides. Drive 4 panel pins through the back board into each side and 2 pins through the back board into the back edge of each shelf. Clean off glue squeeze-out with a damp cloth.
Drill two 6 mm fixing holes in the back board: one 30 mm from the top, one 30 mm from the bottom, centred on the back board width. Countersink each hole on the back face so the screw head sits flush.
Finishing

Sand the rack with 120 then 180 grit. Apply shellac with a soft brush in three thin coats, sanding lightly with 240 grit between coats. Shellac sets quickly; recoat after 4 hours. The rack is ready to fit to the wall the day after the third coat.
Variations
Three-shelf version: extend the back board to 380 mm and add a third shelf at 150 mm from the top. The construction is unchanged.
Oak or beech instead of pine: heavier work for the saw and chisel but a finer-grained rack that holds its colour longer. Same dimensions.
Rounded shelf fronts: a 3 mm round-over on the front edge of each shelf softens the look and matches the front edges of the side boards if you round them off too.
Care

Dust with a soft cloth. Wipe with a damp cloth and dry immediately if the surface gets sticky from kitchen splashes. Re-coat with shellac when the finish dulls; one coat over a cleaned surface restores it.

items.symptom: Rebate is too shallow; shelf stands proud
items.cause: Marking gauge set to less than half the side board thickness, or the chisel did not reach full depth
items.fix: Reset the gauge to 9 mm. Pare the rebate floor with the chisel held bevel-up and the back of the blade against the floor, taking thin shavings until the shelf seats flush.

items.symptom: Saw cut at the rebate shoulder is not square
items.cause: Saw drifted off the line on the first stroke
items.fix: Start the cut with the saw teeth resting on the cross-line and the blade vertical against the try-square. Use the thumb of the off-hand as a guide for the first three strokes.

items.symptom: Glue squeezes out and stains the surface
items.cause: Too much glue or no wiping during clamp-up
items.fix: Use less glue next time; a thin film is enough. Wipe squeeze-out with a damp cloth before it skins over. PVA that has skinned is harder to remove cleanly later.

items.symptom: Back board splits at the nail line
items.cause: Pin driven too close to the edge of the back board, or grain that runs out at the edge
items.fix: Drill a 1 mm pilot hole for each pin first. The pin then follows the hole and does not split the grain.
